_meta.title: SP1 Key Themes Index
_meta.description: Chapter-by-chapter SP1 key themes, structured for cross-referencing with Topic_Frameworks.json and SP1_Revision_Notes.json. Each theme carries keywords, framework topic links, and SP1 notes section references.

schema.sp1_notes_keys: Array of section keys as they appear in SP1_Revision_Notes.json (verify against actual keys)
schema.framework_topics: Array of topic names as they appear in Topic_Frameworks.json (verify against actual keys)
schema.framework_pillars: High-level pillar groupings used in Topic_Frameworks.json
schema.product_cycle_stages: Stages of the product cycle this chapter primarily informs
schema.keywords: Searchable terms extracted from the theme text

verify_against: data/SP1_Revision_Notes.json — confirm chapter section key format (e.g. 'Ch01' vs 'chapter_01')
data/Topic_Frameworks.json — confirm exact topic name strings for framework_topics fields

themes.text: The product cycle: design, pricing, underwriting, claims management, monitoring, valuation
Overview of health and care insurance products
Role of the actuary in health and care insurance
Policy structure: premiums, benefits, deferred periods, linked claims periods
Incapacity definitions: own occupation, any occupation, ADLs, ADWs, FATs
Replacement ratio and prevention of over-insurance
Guaranteed insurability options (GIOs)
Waiver of premium; no-claims discounts; reviewable vs guaranteed premiums
Accelerated vs stand-alone CI insurance
Conditions covered and industry-standard definitions
Tiered / multi-pay benefits
Children's benefit riders; TPD extensions; terminal illness cover
Pre-funded vs immediate needs plans
ADL-based claim triggers; severe mental impairment
Indemnity vs cash benefits; benefit types and structures
Unit-linked and pension-linked LTCI solutions
Surrender benefits; death benefits; deferred periods in LTCI
Indemnity basis; acute vs chronic conditions; standard inclusions and exclusions
Excesses, no-claims discount (NCD) systems, community rating
Cheaper PMI variants: MME, waiting list plans, preferred provider arrangements
Health cash plans; dental and optical plans; personal accident insurance
Definition of group business; employer as policyholder
Free cover limits and eligibility rules
Anti-selection in group schemes; minimum take-up rates
Flexible / cafeteria benefit schemes
Group IP, group CI, group PMI – specific features and differences from individual
Customer needs by product type: IP, CI, LTCI, PMI, microinsurance
Cash vs indemnity benefits; peace of mind value
Insurer constraints: profitability, return on capital, systems, reinsurance, regulation
Product-specific control procedures: underwriting criteria, claims handling, policy wording
Conflicts between stakeholder interests (customer vs insurer vs regulator vs distributor)
Iterative nature of product design and pricing
Channel types: independent brokers, tied agents, own salesforce, direct marketing
Worksite marketing; internet and price comparison sites
Commission structures: indemnity commission, renewal commission, level commission
Clawback on early lapses
Impact of channel on persistency, claims experience and competitiveness
Regulatory regime: restrictions on products, premiums, rating factors, underwriting, solvency
Taxation regime: company tax, tax treatment of premiums and benefits
Economic influences: price inflation, medical inflation, wage inflation, interest rates
Propensity to buy vs need to sell; mis-selling risk; consumer awareness
Professional guidance from actuarial associations
Objectives of State provision: protecting national health, subsidising the poor, budget balance
Methods: universal vs means-tested; flat-rate vs earnings-related; lump sum vs income benefits
Funding: pay-as-you-go vs forward funding
Interaction and complementarity between State provision and private insurance
Drivers of rising healthcare costs: technology, demographics, public expectations
Uses of models: pricing, reserving, solvency, embedded value, model office projections
Deterministic vs stochastic models; key features and outputs
Multi-state modelling for health and care products
Sensitivity testing and scenario analysis
Risk-neutral (market-consistent) vs real-world calibration
Data quality criteria: relevance, credibility, availability, reliability
Sources: own data, population data, reinsurers' data, market/industry data, overseas data
Data requirements by product type (IP, CI, LTCI, PMI)
Claim inception rates, recovery rates, claim amounts – analysis and projection
Purpose of assumptions: pricing, reserving, management accounts
Consistency between assumptions; regulatory and legislative constraints
Margins against adverse experience: assessment approaches, stochastic methods, risk discount rate
Pricing basis vs reserving basis vs management accounts basis
Process: data collection, grouping, rate calculation, adjustment
Claim incidence rates: risk factors, trends, social/economic influences
Claim recovery rates: IP-specific; rehabilitation and claims management effects
Mortality rates: importance by product type
Lapse rates: key influences; selective lapses; lapse and re-entry risk
Benefit amounts and benefit inflation: fixed, index-linked, medical cost inflation
Expenses: types (initial, renewal, claim, termination); expense investigation
Expense inflation: wage and price inflation components
Commission and clawback assumptions: market norms, distribution channel mix
New business volumes and mix: cross-subsidies, per-policy fixed cost allocation
Risk discount rate: shareholders' required return, CAPM, risk premium
Market consistency: risk-neutral valuations, risk margins
IP pricing: inception/annuity approach; multi-state modelling
CI pricing: incidence rates by cause; accelerated vs stand-alone formulae
LTCI pricing: multiple-state or inception/annuity methods
PMI pricing: risk premium formula by treatment category
Health cash plans and accident & sickness pricing
Modelling complexities: claim frequency and amount distributions
Free cover limits and group-level rating factors
Book rate calculation: data judgement, risk cells, inflation adjustments
Experience rating: credibility formula, credibility factor Z
Burning cost method: calculation and use for monitoring premium adequacy
Group PMI pricing specifics: NCD, flat rating, self-insurance
Profit criteria: NPV, IRR, discounted payback period
Marketability and competitiveness; loss-leader strategies
Cost of holding supervisory reserves and solvency capital in pricing
Reinsurance impact on pricing
Regulatory constraints on pricing bases and rating factors
Premium rate reviews and monitoring
Mortality/morbidity options: GIOs, convertibility, indexation options
Valuing options: cashflow projections; key assumptions (take-up rate, morbidity of exercisers)
Cost of guarantees: expense, claim and investment guarantees
Sensitivity testing of guaranteed products; capital cost implications
Reasons for calculating reserves: accounts, solvency, pricing, M&A, investment strategy
Types of reserve: long-term (in-force, IBNR, option reserves) and short-term (UPR, URR, IBNER, outstanding claims)
Methods: case estimates vs statistical methods; use cases for each
Embedded value and appraisal value; use in M&A
Solvency capital requirements: formula-based vs VaR-based approaches
Stress testing; aggregation and diversification (correlation matrices, copulas)
Market-consistent valuation: risk-free rates, risk margins, cost of capital approach
Active vs passive valuation approaches
Model risk, parameter risk, random fluctuation risk
Policy and other data risks; model office accuracy
Claims risks: new diseases, medical advances, anti-selection, PMI claim cost inflation
Investment performance risk: model/parameter/random components
Expense risk; persistency risk; selective lapses
Guarantees and options risk
Competition and management action risks
Counterparty risks: reinsurers, distributors, medical service providers
Regulatory and fiscal development risks
Reputational risks; internal audit failure and fraud; physical risks
Catastrophe and concentration of risk; non-disclosure and anti-selection
Product-specific risk profiles: IP, CI, LTCI, PMI
Aims of management: profit, return on capital, risk profile, public interest
Problems confronting the actuary: data, design, marketability, pricing, capital, solvency, investment, claims
Purposes of reinsurance: risk limitation, large loss protection, smoothing, expertise, capacity, financing, arbitrage
Treaty vs facultative; fac/fac, fac/ob, ob/ob arrangements
Proportional reinsurance: quota share and surplus; original terms vs risk premium basis
Non-proportional reinsurance: risk XL, aggregate XL (stop loss), catastrophe XL
Financial reinsurance: new business financing, loan-like structures
Reinsurance commission and rebate structures
Underwriting: full medical underwriting, moratorium underwriting, MHD
Claims management and product-specific controls (IP: disability counselling; PMI: pre-authorisation)
Data quality controls: vetting, spot checks, compulsory fields, staff training
Product design as a risk control tool: claim definitions, policy wording, guarantees
Distribution management: monitoring sales message, commission controls, churning
Treating Customers Fairly (TCF): clear policy conditions, sales process monitoring
Asset-liability matching by liability type: monetary, index-linked, indemnity, investment-linked
Immunisation theory and its limitations for monetary liabilities
Regulatory constraints on investment (asset types, counterparty limits, currency matching)
Asset-liability modelling: testing investment strategies, probability of insolvency
Overall investment strategy: maximising return subject to acceptable risk; free assets
Asset valuation: market value vs discounted cashflow approaches
Purposes of experience monitoring: updating assumptions, identifying adverse trends, management information
Experience investigations: new business, mortality/morbidity, claims amounts, persistency, expenses, investment
New business analysis: strain, mix vs pricing basis, commission adequacy, staffing
Expense investigations: allocation to initial, renewal, termination and investment costs
Analysis of surplus (long-term) and claim/expense ratios (short-term)
Embedded value analysis: reconciliation, management information, regulatory reporting
Feeding results back into: pricing, reserving, underwriting, distribution, product design, capital allocation
